hero.title: Multi-platform Profit Calculator for Ecommerce Sellers
hero.subtitle: Compare the same product across Shopify, Etsy, eBay, Amazon, and TikTok Shop using editable fee assumptions.

notice.title: Editable assumptions
notice.body: Fee assumptions are editable estimates. Platform fees may vary by country, category, account type, payment method, and policy changes. Fee assumptions last reviewed: 2026-06.

multiPlatform.refundReserveNote: Refund reserve is estimated conservatively from net revenue. It is a planning reserve, not a prediction of exact refund losses.

feeFields.storageOrOtherFeePerUnit: Storage or other fee per unit

validation.nonNegative: Enter zero or a positive number.
validation.positive: Enter a number greater than zero.
validation.percentLikelyFraction: Did you mean 30%? Enter 30 for thirty percent.
validation.percentVeryHigh: This percentage is very high. Please confirm it is correct.
validation.netRevenueNonPositive: Net revenue is 0 or negative after discount. Lower the discount or increase the selling price before comparing margins.

formulas: Discount amount = Selling price x Discount rate
Net revenue = Selling price - Discount amount
Refund reserve = Net revenue x Refund rate
Platform fee = Net revenue x platform fee percentage + fixed platform fees. TikTok Shop can also include an editable affiliate commission.
Payment fee = Net revenue x payment fee percentage + fixed payment fee
Total cost = product cost + shipping + packaging + ad cost + refund reserve + platform fee + payment fee + fulfillment fee + other variable cost
Net profit = Net revenue - Total cost
Profit margin = Net profit / Net revenue x 100
Gross revenue ROAS = Selling price / Ad cost; net revenue ROAS = Net revenue / Ad cost; POAS = Net profit / Ad cost. If ad cost is 0, all three are N/A.

example.body: If a product sells for 49.99 with a 10% discount and 8.00 ad cost per order, this calculator first finds net revenue, then subtracts product cost, seller-paid shipping, packaging, refund reserve, ad cost, and the editable fee assumptions for each platform.

mistakes: Treating default fee assumptions as official rates instead of editable estimates.
Forgetting fixed order, listing, payment, or fulfillment fees.
Ignoring discounts, refunds, and seller-paid shipping.
Comparing ROAS without checking whether the order is profitable after platform fees.

faq.question: Why is my profit different across platforms?
faq.answer: Each marketplace can have different referral, transaction, payment, listing, fulfillment, and promotional fee assumptions.

faq.question: Are the platform fee assumptions accurate?
faq.answer: They are editable planning assumptions, not official long-term fee schedules. Verify important numbers with each platform.

faq.question: Does this include VAT, sales tax, or income tax?
faq.answer: No. This calculator is for operating margin estimates and does not provide tax, accounting, legal, or financial advice.

faq.question: How should I estimate ad cost per order?
faq.answer: Use your average cost to acquire one order from paid ads, or test several scenarios before scaling spend.

faq.question: What is a good profit margin for ecommerce sellers?
faq.answer: It depends on category, fulfillment model, returns, ad intensity, and growth stage. Use the calculator to compare scenarios rather than relying on a single benchmark.

pro.modalBody: This Pro feature is coming soon. Join the waitlist to get early access.

hero.title: Break-even ROAS and Ad Spend Calculator
hero.subtitle: Estimate how much you can spend on ads, what ROAS breaks even, and what ROAS is needed to hit a target profit.

notice.title: Editable estimate
notice.body: Fee and cost assumptions are editable estimates. Platform, payment, advertising, refund, and fulfillment costs can vary by country, account, category, and policy changes. Verify important numbers with current official sources.

warnings.revenueNonPositive: Selling price must be greater than zero before ROAS or margin can be calculated.
warnings.contributionMarginNonPositive: This product is not profitable before advertising based on your current inputs.
warnings.targetAdSpendNonPositive: Your target profit is higher than the available contribution margin. You may need to increase price, reduce costs, or lower the target margin.

validation.nonNegative: Enter zero or a positive number.
validation.sellingPricePositive: Selling price must be greater than zero.
validation.percentLikelyFraction: Did you mean 30%? Enter 30 for thirty percent.
validation.percentVeryHigh: This percentage is very high. Please confirm it is correct.

statuses.notAvailable: Enter current ROAS to evaluate status.

formulas: Revenue = Selling price
Platform fee = Revenue x platform fee percentage
Payment fee = Revenue x payment fee percentage + fixed payment fee
Variable cost before ads = product cost + shipping + packaging + platform fee + payment fee + other variable cost
Contribution margin before ads = Revenue - variable cost before ads
Break-even ad spend per order = Contribution margin before ads
Max CPA at break-even = Break-even ad spend per order
Break-even ROAS = Revenue / break-even ad spend per order
Target profit = fixed target profit per order, or Revenue x target profit margin
Target ad spend per order = Contribution margin before ads - target profit
Target ROAS = Revenue / target ad spend per order
Break-even ACoS = Break-even ad spend per order / Revenue
Target ACoS = Target ad spend per order / Revenue

example.body: If revenue is 50.00 and variable cost before ads is 30.00, contribution margin before ads is 20.00. That means break-even ad spend and max CPA are 20.00. If target profit is 8.00, target ad spend is 12.00 and target ROAS is revenue divided by 12.00.

mistakes: Using ROAS without subtracting platform and payment fees first.
Forgetting fixed payment fees or other variable costs.
Setting a target profit higher than the available contribution margin.
Treating break-even ROAS as a target instead of the minimum line before losses.

faq.question: What is break-even ROAS?
faq.answer: Break-even ROAS is the ROAS where revenue divided by ad spend just covers the variable cost structure before profit.

faq.question: What is the difference between ROAS and ACoS?
faq.answer: ROAS is revenue divided by ad spend. ACoS is ad spend divided by revenue. They describe the same relationship from opposite directions.

faq.question: How do I calculate max CPA?
faq.answer: For one order, max CPA at break-even is the contribution margin before ads.

faq.question: Why is my target ROAS so high?
faq.answer: Target ROAS rises when costs are high, contribution margin is low, or the target profit is close to the available margin.

faq.question: How can I lower my break-even ROAS?
faq.answer: Increase price, reduce product or fulfillment costs, reduce platform/payment fees, or improve conversion so each order can support more ad spend.

hero.title: Stripe vs PayPal Fee Calculator for Online Sellers
hero.subtitle: Compare editable Stripe, PayPal, and Shopify Payments fee assumptions to estimate processing fees, net received, and savings for online orders.

notice.title: Editable fee assumptions
notice.body: Fee assumptions are editable estimates. Payment processor fees vary by country, currency, card type, account type, transaction type, and policy updates. Always verify current rates with each provider. Fee assumptions last reviewed: 2026-06.

validation.nonNegative: Enter zero or a positive number.
validation.positive: Enter a number greater than zero.
validation.positiveInteger: Enter a whole number greater than zero.
validation.percentLikelyFraction: Did you mean 30%? Enter 30 for thirty percent.
validation.percentVeryHigh: This percentage is very high. Please confirm it is correct.
validation.transactionAmountNonPositive: Transaction amount must be greater than zero.
validation.numberOfTransactionsNonPositive: Number of transactions must be greater than zero.
validation.extremePercentage: One or more percentage assumptions are 1000% or higher. Check whether the values are intentional.

paymentFees.riskNote: Refund and chargeback estimates include only the editable payment-side costs entered here. They do not estimate refunded revenue, lost product, shipping, recovery, or provider-specific policy effects.
paymentFees.refundRetainedPercentageFeeTooltip: Refund retained percentage fee is an editable assumption for the percentage fee a payment provider keeps or does not refund. Rules vary by provider and region.
formulas: Gross transaction volume = Transaction amount x Number of transactions
Base fee per transaction = Transaction amount x Percentage fee + Fixed fee
Extra percentage fee per transaction = Transaction amount x (international surcharge + currency conversion fee + optional extra fee)
Estimated refund cost = Estimated refunded volume x Refund retained percentage fee + Estimated refunded transactions x Non-refundable fixed fee per refund
Estimated chargeback cost = Number of transactions x Chargeback rate x Fixed fee per chargeback
Total fees = (Base fee + Extra percentage fee) x Number of transactions + Estimated refund cost + Estimated chargeback cost
Net received = Gross transaction volume - Total fees
Effective fee rate = Total fees / Gross transaction volume x 100
Lowest-cost provider = provider with the lowest total fees
Savings = Highest total fees - Lowest total fees
Monthly estimated fees = Total fees
Annual estimated fees = Monthly estimated fees x 12

example.body: If each order is 50.00 and there are 500 transactions, gross transaction volume is 25,000.00. A 2.9% fee plus a 0.30 fixed fee creates a per-transaction fee of 1.75 before any optional extra percentage, then total monthly fees are that amount multiplied by 500.

mistakes: Treating editable assumptions as official current rates.
Forgetting fixed fees when average order value is low.
Comparing only percentage fees while ignoring currency, card type, account type, and transaction type.
Ignoring refunds, chargebacks, and currency conversion policies that may change final cost.

faq.question: Why do Stripe and PayPal fees differ?
faq.answer: Payment providers can price differently based on country, currency, card network, account type, transaction type, risk rules, and policy changes.

faq.question: Are these payment fee assumptions official?
faq.answer: No. They are editable planning assumptions, not official fee schedules. Always verify current rates with each provider.

faq.question: Do these fees include currency conversion?
faq.answer: Only if you enter an estimated currency conversion cost in the optional extra fee percentage. Currency conversion rules vary by provider and transaction.

faq.question: Do refunds and chargebacks change the final cost?
faq.answer: They can. This calculator keeps refund and chargeback rates visible for scenario planning, but it does not hard-code provider-specific refund or chargeback penalties.

faq.question: How can online sellers reduce payment processing fees?
faq.answer: Compare providers, increase average order value, reduce refunds and chargebacks, review account pricing, and verify whether local payment methods or plans lower your effective fee rate.

hero.title: Landed Cost Calculator for Importers and Ecommerce Sellers
hero.subtitle: Estimate the true unit cost of a product from supplier purchase through freight, customs, warehouse preparation, and selling costs.

notice.title: Estimation notice
notice.body: This calculator is for estimation only. Duty, VAT, GST, customs rules, and shipping charges vary by country, product category, HS code, and policy updates. Always verify important numbers with official customs sources or a qualified professional. Fee assumptions last reviewed: 2026-06.

inputs.warehousePrepCostPerUnit: Warehouse or prep cost per unit

landedCost.customsValuationNote: Choose the planning basis required for your scenario. Actual customs valuation rules vary by country and product.
landedCost.importTaxTreatmentNote: If VAT/GST is recoverable, select No to show the tax without adding it to landed cost. Confirm the treatment with a qualified professional.

outputs.importTaxPerUnit: Import tax / VAT / GST per unit
outputs.fixedCostPerUnit: Fixed cost per unit before percentage-based selling costs

validation.nonNegative: Enter zero or a positive number.
validation.positiveInteger: Enter a whole number greater than zero.
validation.invalidFreightType: Select Sea, Air, Express, or Other.
validation.percentLikelyFraction: Did you mean 30%? Enter 30 for thirty percent.
validation.percentVeryHigh: This percentage is very high. Please confirm it is correct.
validation.quantityNonPositive: Quantity must be greater than zero.
validation.breakEvenDenominatorNonPositive: Platform, payment, and refund percentages leave no valid break-even price. Reduce the combined variable rate below 100%.
validation.breakEvenDenominatorNearZero: The variable fee rate is extremely high, so the calculated selling price may be unrealistic.
validation.targetDenominatorNonPositive: The target margin plus variable selling rates must be below 100% to calculate a suggested price.

formulas: Total product cost = Product unit cost x Quantity
Freight per unit = International freight cost / Quantity
Insurance per unit = Insurance cost / Quantity
CIF customs value = Product unit cost + Freight per unit + Insurance per unit; FOB customs value = Product unit cost
Duty per unit = Customs value per unit x Customs duty rate
Import tax per unit = (Customs value per unit + Duty per unit) x Import tax rate
Broker fee per unit = Customs broker fee / Quantity
Other import fees per unit = Other import fees / Quantity
Landed cost per unit = Product unit cost + freight + insurance + duty + included import tax + broker fee + other import fees
Selling fees per unit = Selling price x Platform fee percentage + Selling price x Payment fee percentage + Fixed payment fee
Refund reserve = Selling price x Refund rate
Total cost per unit = Landed cost + selling fees + ad cost + refund reserve + domestic delivery + packaging + warehouse/prep + other cost
Net profit per unit = Selling price - Total cost per unit
Profit margin = Net profit per unit / Selling price x 100
Fixed cost per unit excludes platform fee, percentage payment fee, and refund reserve
Variable selling rate = Platform fee rate + Payment fee rate + Refund rate
Break-even selling price = Fixed cost per unit / (1 - Variable selling rate)
Suggested price for target margin = Fixed cost per unit / (1 - Variable selling rate - Target margin)

example.body: For 100 units at 10.00 each, 500.00 freight, and 100.00 insurance, freight is 5.00 per unit and insurance is 1.00 per unit. Customs value is 16.00 per unit. At 5% duty, duty is 0.80. At 10% import tax, tax is 1.68 before deciding whether VAT/GST is included in landed cost.

mistakes: Dividing freight or broker fees by the wrong quantity.
Applying duty only to product cost while customs value may also include freight and insurance.
Automatically treating recoverable VAT or GST as a permanent cost.
Pricing from landed cost alone while ignoring payment fees, advertising, refunds, domestic delivery, and preparation costs.

faq.question: What is landed cost?
faq.answer: Landed cost is the estimated unit cost after product purchase, international freight, insurance, duty, selected import tax treatment, customs brokerage, and other import fees.

faq.question: Why does landed cost matter for ecommerce sellers?
faq.answer: It shows the cost of getting inventory ready for sale, helping sellers avoid pricing products from supplier cost alone.

faq.question: How do I find my customs duty rate?
faq.answer: Use the product HS code and verify the applicable rate with the destination country's official customs source or a qualified customs professional.

faq.question: Should VAT or GST be included as cost?
faq.answer: It depends on whether the tax is recoverable, your registration status, local rules, and accounting treatment. Confirm with a qualified professional.

faq.question: What is the difference between landed cost and total selling cost?
faq.answer: Landed cost covers getting the product imported and ready. Total selling cost also includes platform, payment, advertising, refunds, domestic delivery, packaging, warehouse, and other per-order costs.

about.title: Practical planning tools for ecommerce sellers
about.intro: SellerMargin is a free calculator site built to help ecommerce sellers understand the numbers behind everyday pricing and growth decisions.

sections.title: What the site helps you estimate
paragraphs: The calculators help sellers estimate product profit, advertising break-even points, platform fees, payment processing fees, and landed cost.
bullets: Compare estimated profit across ecommerce platforms.
Calculate break-even ROAS, target ROAS, Max CPA, and ACoS.
Estimate import, customs, freight, and selling costs per unit.

sections.title: No account required in the first version
paragraphs: The first version is designed to work without registration. Recent calculator inputs may be saved locally in your browser so you can continue a scenario after refreshing the page.

sections.title: Planning estimates, not professional advice
paragraphs: Calculator results are for planning and estimation only. SellerMargin does not provide financial, tax, legal, or accounting advice. Important numbers should be verified with official sources or a qualified professional.

sections.title: Possible future Pro features
paragraphs: Future versions may add optional paid workflow features. These features are not active in the current version.

contact.title: Contact SellerMargin
contact.intro: Use this page for general questions, corrections, or feedback about the calculators and guides.
contact.emailNotice: To contact us, email the address above. This form is currently a front-end demo and does not send messages.
contact.formNotice: This form is a visual demo only. It does not send data to a server or email service.
contact.demoNotice: Demo only: submitting this form does not send email and does not store the message in a server or database.
contact.success: Demo success. No message was sent or stored.

privacyPolicy.title: Privacy Policy
privacyPolicy.intro: This policy describes the limited data handling used in the first version of SellerMargin.

sections.title: Accounts and calculator processing
paragraphs: The first version does not require an account. Calculator inputs and results are generally processed in your browser.

sections.title: Local browser storage
paragraphs: SellerMargin may use localStorage to remember recent calculator inputs, language preferences, and demo waitlist status on your device.
bullets: This information remains in the browser unless you clear site data.
The first version does not use localStorage to store passwords or payment information.
Demo contact and waitlist forms do not send messages or email addresses to a server or database.

sections.title: Information not collected
paragraphs: SellerMargin does not collect payment card information and does not store account passwords in this version.

sections.title: Possible future analytics
paragraphs: V1 does not use non-essential cookies, analytics, or advertising tracking.

If a future version adds analytics, email services, payments, accounts, advertising tracking, or other third-party scripts, this policy must be updated and cookie consent must be enabled where required.

sections.title: Contact
paragraphs: Users may contact the site owner through the Contact page with privacy questions or requests.

terms.title: Terms of Use
terms.intro: These terms describe the conditions for using SellerMargin calculators, guides, and planning estimates.

sections.title: As-is service
paragraphs: By using this website, you agree to these terms.
SellerMargin tools and content are provided on an "as-is" and "as-available" basis. Features may change, be corrected, or be removed without notice.

sections.title: No guarantee of accuracy
paragraphs: SellerMargin does not guarantee that calculations, default assumptions, fees, formulas, or guide content are complete, current, or error-free.

sections.title: Your business decisions
paragraphs: You are responsible for reviewing inputs and making your own pricing, advertising, importing, platform, and business decisions.
bullets: Verify fees, taxes, duties, policies, and rates with official sources.
Consult qualified professionals when a decision requires financial, tax, legal, or accounting expertise.

sections.title: No professional advice
paragraphs: SellerMargin does not provide financial, tax, legal, or accounting advice.

sections.title: Limitation of responsibility
paragraphs: To the extent permitted by applicable law, the site owner is not responsible for business losses or decisions made in reliance on estimates produced by the site.

disclaimer.title: Calculator and Content Disclaimer
disclaimer.intro: SellerMargin provides estimation tools and educational content for planning purposes only.

sections.title: Estimates only
paragraphs: All calculator outputs are estimates based on the information and assumptions entered by the user.

sections.title: Costs and rules can change
paragraphs: Platform fees, payment fees, taxes, customs duties, shipping charges, and advertising costs may change.
bullets: Rules may vary by country and jurisdiction.
Platforms may apply different rules by category or account type.
Payment methods and transaction types may have different fee structures.
Default fee values are editable assumptions and are not official rates guaranteed to remain accurate over time.

sections.title: Verify important information
paragraphs: Users should verify important assumptions through official platform, payment provider, customs, tax, and shipping sources or consult a qualified professional.

sections.title: No professional advice
paragraphs: This website does not provide legal, tax, financial, or accounting advice.

guides.title: Ecommerce profit guides
guides.intro: Practical explanations for understanding costs, contribution margin, advertising efficiency, and pricing decisions.
guides.articleTitle: How to Calculate Ecommerce Profit After Ads
guides.articleDescription: A practical walkthrough of the costs to include, the basic formula, and common mistakes when measuring profit after advertising.
guides.toolsIntro: Open any calculator directly to compare platforms, advertising targets, payment fees, or landed costs.
guides.futureBody: Additional guides may be added later. No unpublished articles or results are being presented as completed content.

profitAfterAdsGuide.title: How to Calculate Ecommerce Profit After Ads
profitAfterAdsGuide.intro: Revenue and gross profit can look healthy while advertising, payment, platform, refunds, and fulfillment costs quietly reduce the amount left from each order.

introduction.title: 1. Introduction
paragraphs: Profit after ads is the amount left after subtracting the costs required to produce, sell, fulfill, and acquire an order. It is more useful for day-to-day decisions than revenue alone.
A practical calculation starts with real order revenue and uses editable assumptions that match your product, platform, payment method, fulfillment model, and advertising performance.

Calculate at the order or unit level first. Once the unit economics are clear, you can multiply them by expected order volume to build a monthly plan without hiding an unprofitable order inside a large revenue total.

grossProfit.title: 2. Why gross profit is not enough
paragraphs: Gross profit often subtracts only product cost from revenue. That can be useful, but it does not show whether the order remains profitable after selling and customer-acquisition costs.
A product can have a strong gross margin and still lose money after platform fees, payment fees, shipping subsidies, refunds, advertising, and import costs are included.

Contribution margin is usually the more useful bridge between gross profit and final operating profit. It shows how much remains after the variable costs attached to an order, so you can see how much advertising spend the order can support.

keyCosts.title: 3. Key costs to include
keyCosts.intro: Use costs that belong to one completed order, and keep assumptions consistent.

items.title: Product cost
items.body: The supplier or manufacturing cost for the units sold in the order.

items.title: Shipping
items.body: Include seller-paid delivery, fulfillment, postage, or shipping subsidies.

items.title: Platform fees
items.body: Marketplace commissions, transaction charges, listing-related costs, or other platform deductions.

items.title: Payment fees
items.body: Percentage and fixed fees charged by the payment processor.

items.title: Refund reserve
items.body: An estimated allowance for expected refunds and related unrecovered costs.

items.title: Ad spend
items.body: The average advertising cost required to acquire one order, sometimes called CPA.

items.title: Import or landed costs
items.body: Freight, insurance, duty, selected import tax treatment, brokerage, and other import costs allocated per unit.

formula.title: 4. Basic formula
lines: Profit after ads = Net revenue - product cost - shipping - platform fees - payment fees - refund reserve - ad spend - other variable costs
Profit margin = Profit after ads / Net revenue x 100

example.title: 5. Example calculation
paragraphs: Suppose an order produces 50.00 in net revenue. Product cost is 16.00, shipping and packaging are 6.00, platform and payment fees are 6.50, refund reserve is 1.50, and ad spend is 10.00.

The example leaves 10.00 after variable costs, which is a 20% margin on net revenue. That does not automatically mean the business earns 20% overall: salaries, software, rent, tax, and other overhead may still need to be paid from that contribution.

calculators.title: 6. How to use the calculators on this site
calculators.intro: Start with the calculator that matches the largest uncertainty in your order economics. Use the results as planning scenarios, compare a conservative and an optimistic case, then verify important assumptions before acting.

links.title: Multi-platform Profit Calculator
links.body: Compare estimated order profit across editable platform fee scenarios.

links.title: Break-even ROAS and Ad Spend Calculator
links.body: Find break-even ROAS, target ROAS, Max CPA, and ACoS.

links.title: Stripe vs PayPal Fee Calculator
links.body: Compare editable payment processing assumptions and net received.

links.title: Landed Cost Calculator
links.body: Allocate freight, customs, import, warehouse, and selling costs per unit.

mistakes.title: 7. Common mistakes
items: Using revenue or gross profit as if it were final profit.
Ignoring fixed payment fees, refunds, packaging, or seller-paid shipping.
Using an average ROAS without connecting it to contribution margin.
Treating editable fee assumptions as guaranteed official rates.
Forgetting that import, tax, and platform rules can change.

items.question: Should I calculate profit using gross or net revenue?
items.answer: Use net revenue after discounts for the margin denominator, and separately include refunds or refund reserves so the assumptions remain visible.

items.question: Is ROAS the same as profit?
items.answer: No. ROAS compares revenue with ad spend, while profit also subtracts product, fulfillment, platform, payment, refund, and other operating costs.

items.question: How often should I update the calculation?
items.answer: Update it whenever prices, advertising costs, fees, shipping, refund behavior, or supplier costs change materially.

items.question: Should I include overhead in every order?
items.answer: This guide focuses on variable order economics. You can allocate overhead per order for a fuller operating view, but keep that assumption separate and consistent.

cta.title: Turn the formula into a working scenario
cta.body: Use the calculators to test your current numbers, then save a separate record of the assumptions you verified with platforms, payment providers, carriers, or customs sources.
cta.label: Open the profit calculator

disclaimer.title: 9. Disclaimer
paragraphs: This guide and the calculators are for planning and estimation only. They do not promise profit and do not provide tax, legal, financial, or accounting advice.
Verify important fees, tax treatment, customs rules, and business assumptions with official sources or qualified professionals.
